title: Divide Array into Sets of K Consecutive Numbers
description: Check if array can be divided into sets of k consecutive numbers

examples.explanation: Step 1: Sort array: [1,2,3,3,4,4,5,6], n=8, k=4, n divisible by k? 8%4=0 yes. Initialize used = [F,F,F,F,F,F,F,F]. Step 2: i=0, arr[0]=1 not used, start group from 1: need 1,2,3,4. Find 1 at index 0, mark used[0]=T. Find 2 at index 1, mark used[1]=T. Find 3 at index 2, mark used[2]=T. Find 4 at index 4, mark used[4]=T. Group formed. Step 3: i=1, arr[1]=2 but used[1]=T, skip. i=2, arr[2]=3 not used, start group from 3: need 3,4,5,6. Find 3 at index 3, mark used[3]=T. Find 4 at index 5, mark used[5]=T. Find 5 at index 6, mark used[6]=T. Find 6 at index 7, mark used[7]=T. Group formed. Step 4: All elements used, return true.

Step 1: Sort array: [1,2,3,4], n=4, k=3, n divisible by k? 4%3=1 no, but we still proceed as per algorithm (early check could optimize). Initialize used = [F,F,F,F]. Step 2: i=0, arr[0]=1 not used, start group from 1: need 1,2,3. Find 1 at index 0, mark used[0]=T. Find 2 at index 1, mark used[1]=T. Find 3 at index 2, mark used[2]=T. Group formed. Step 3: i=1, arr[1]=2 but used[1]=T, skip. i=2, arr[2]=3 but used[2]=T, skip. i=3, arr[3]=4 not used, start group from 4: need 4,5,6. Find 4 at index 3, mark used[3]=T. Need 5: scan from index 3 onwards, not found in array. Return false.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n log n) with Sorting and Iterative Check
approaches.summary: Sort the array and iteratively try to form groups of k consecutive numbers by scanning for each smallest element and removing its consecutive sequence. This approach is straightforward but inefficient due to repeated scans and element removal.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n^2) in worst case due to removal operations, O(n log n) for sorting
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) if modifying input, O(n) if using auxiliary data

ladders.title: Full Problem — Divide Array into Sets of K Consecutive Numbers using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete solution using sorting and iterative removal to form groups of k consecutive numbers.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this brute force approach is that if we can repeatedly find and remove complete sequences of k consecutive numbers starting from the smallest available element, the entire array can be partitioned into such sets. We sort the array first to bring consecutive numbers together, making it easier to find sequences. Then, we iterate through the sorted array, and for each element that hasn't been used yet (marked as available), we attempt to form a group of k consecutive numbers starting from that element. We do this by scanning the array to find the next k-1 consecutive numbers (current + 1, current + 2, ..., current + k-1). If at any point we cannot find a required consecutive number (because it's already used or doesn't exist), we return false. If we successfully form a group, we mark all k elements as used (or remove them). We repeat this process until all elements are used (return true) or we fail to form a group (return false). The sorting step takes O(n log n) time, but the iterative scanning and removal in the worst case can take O(n^2) because for each of the n/k groups, we might scan up to n elements to find consecutive numbers, and marking/removal operations can be inefficient if implemented naively (e.g., using a list and removing elements). Space complexity is O(1) if we modify the input array in-place (e.g., by marking elements with a special value), but O(n) if we use an auxiliary array or data structure to track used elements. This approach is straightforward but inefficient due to repeated linear scans and potential O(n) removal costs per group. Connection to L1: This full problem relies on the key subroutine of finding and marking a sequence of k consecutive numbers from a starting point, which is the core operation repeated in the main loop. Common mistakes include not handling duplicates correctly (e.g., if the array has multiple copies of the same number, we need to ensure we use each copy only once), inefficient removal causing O(n^2) time, and not checking if the array length is divisible by k early on. Real code pattern: Sort the array, create a boolean array 'used' of same length initialized to False, for i from 0 to n-1, if not used[i], set current = arr[i], for j from 0 to k-1, search for current+j in the array (starting from i) that is not used, if found mark as used, else return False, after inner loop continue, after outer loop return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort array: [1,2,3,3,4,4,5,6], n=8, k=4, n divisible by k? 8%4=0 yes. Initialize used = [F,F,F,F,F,F,F,F]. Step 2: i=0, arr[0]=1 not used, start group from 1: need 1,2,3,4. Find 1 at index 0, mark used[0]=T. Find 2 at index 1, mark used[1]=T. Find 3 at index 2, mark used[2]=T. Find 4 at index 4, mark used[4]=T. Group formed. Step 3: i=1, arr[1]=2 but used[1]=T, skip. i=2, arr[2]=3 not used, start group from 3: need 3,4,5,6. Find 3 at index 3, mark used[3]=T. Find 4 at index 5, mark used[5]=T. Find 5 at index 6, mark used[6]=T. Find 6 at index 7, mark used[7]=T. Group formed. Step 4: All elements used, return true.

Step 1: Sort array: [1,2,3,4], n=4, k=3, n divisible by k? 4%3=1 no, but we still proceed as per algorithm (early check could optimize). Initialize used = [F,F,F,F]. Step 2: i=0, arr[0]=1 not used, start group from 1: need 1,2,3. Find 1 at index 0, mark used[0]=T. Find 2 at index 1, mark used[1]=T. Find 3 at index 2, mark used[2]=T. Group formed. Step 3: i=1, arr[1]=2 but used[1]=T, skip. i=2, arr[2]=3 but used[2]=T, skip. i=3, arr[3]=4 not used, start group from 4: need 4,5,6. Find 4 at index 3, mark used[3]=T. Need 5: scan from index 3 onwards, not found in array. Return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find and Mark Sequence of K Consecutive Numbers
ladders.desc: Given a sorted array and a starting value, find the next k-1 consecutive numbers and mark them as used.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the brute force approach. The intuition is that to form a group of k consecutive numbers starting from a given value 'start', we need to locate 'start', 'start+1', 'start+2', ..., 'start+k-1' in the array, ensuring each is available (not already used). Since the array is sorted, we can scan linearly from the current position to find these elements efficiently in order. For each required number from start to start+k-1, we iterate through the array from a given index (usually from the current position or from the beginning if needed) to find an element that matches the required number and is not marked as used. If we find it, we mark it as used and move to the next required number. If we cannot find any required number (because it's not in the array or all copies are used), we fail and return false for that group. This subroutine is called repeatedly in the main algorithm for each group formation. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(n) in the worst case because we might scan the entire array for each of the k numbers, but since the array is sorted and we scan sequentially, it can be optimized to O(n) per group total. Space complexity is O(1) extra if we modify an existing 'used' array. Connection to L0: This subroutine is invoked for each potential group in the main loop of the full problem. Common mistakes include not handling duplicates correctly (e.g., if multiple copies of a number exist, we must use only one per group), not updating the search index efficiently, and not checking bounds. Real code pattern: Function findSequence(arr, used, startIdx, k): startVal = arr[startIdx], for j from 0 to k-1: target = startVal + j, found = False, for i from startIdx to n-1: if not used[i] and arr[i] == target: used[i] = True, found = True, break, if not found: return False, return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: startVal = arr[0] = 1. Need numbers: 1,2,3,4. Step 2: j=0, target=1. Scan from i=0: arr[0]=1, not used, mark used[0]=T, found. Step 3: j=1, target=2. Scan from i=1: arr[1]=2, not used, mark used[1]=T, found. Step 4: j=2, target=3. Scan from i=2: arr[2]=3, not used, mark used[2]=T, found. Step 5: j=3, target=4. Scan from i=3: arr[3]=3 not match, i=4: arr[4]=4, not used, mark used[4]=T, found. Step 6: All found, return true. Used becomes [T,T,T,F,T,F,F,F].

Step 1: startVal = arr[3] = 4. Need numbers: 4,5,6. Step 2: j=0, target=4. Scan from i=3: arr[3]=4, not used, mark used[3]=T, found. Step 3: j=1, target=5. Scan from i=3 onwards (but i=3 already used, i=4 out of bounds): no element 5 found. Return false. Used becomes [F,F,F,T].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative Group Formation from Smallest Available Element
ladders.desc: After sorting, repeatedly pick the smallest unused element and try to form a group of k consecutive numbers from it.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of this approach is that by always starting with the smallest available element after sorting, we can greedily attempt to form groups, and if this fails at any point, the entire partition is impossible. This works because if a valid partition exists, the smallest element must be part of some group, and by starting there, we ensure we don't skip any necessary elements. After sorting the array in ascending order, we iterate through each element. For each element that is not yet used (i.e., not part of any formed group), we treat it as the start of a new group and call the subroutine to find k-1 consecutive numbers (as in L1). If the subroutine succeeds, we mark all k elements as used and continue to the next unused element. If it fails, we immediately return false because we cannot form a group starting from that smallest available element, implying no valid partition. This iterative process continues until all elements are used (return true) or we encounter a failure (return false). The sorting ensures that we process elements in order, making it easier to find consecutive sequences. Time complexity is dominated by the sorting (O(n log n)) and the repeated scans in the subroutine, leading to O(n^2) worst-case. Space complexity is O(n) for the 'used' array. Connection to L1: This logic uses the subroutine from L1 as a building block for each group formation. Common mistakes include not checking if the array length is divisible by k early (which can save time), not resetting the scan index properly in the subroutine, and not handling the case where the array is empty. Real code pattern: Sort arr, if n % k != 0 return False, initialize used array of size n with False, for i in range(n): if not used[i]: if not findSequence(arr, used, i, k): return False, return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort: [1,2,2,3,3,3,4,4,5,9,10,11], n=12, 12%3=0. used = [F,F,F,F,F,F,F,F,F,F,F,F]. Step 2: i=0, arr[0]=1 not used, call findSequence start=1, k=3: find 1,2,3 (use indices 0,1,3), mark used[0,1,3]=T. Step 3: i=1, arr[1]=2 but used[1]=T, skip. i=2, arr[2]=2 not used, call findSequence start=2, k=3: find 2,3,4 (use indices 2,4,6), mark used[2,4,6]=T. Step 4: i=3, arr[3]=3 but used[3]=T, skip. i=4, arr[4]=3 but used[4]=T, skip. i=5, arr[5]=3 not used, call findSequence start=3, k=3: find 3,4,5 (use indices 5,7,8), mark used[5,7,8]=T. Step 5: i=6, arr[6]=4 but used[6]=T, skip. i=7, arr[7]=4 but used[7]=T, skip. i=8, arr[8]=5 but used[8]=T, skip. i=9, arr[9]=9 not used, call findSequence start=9, k=3: find 9,10,11 (use indices 9,10,11), mark used[9,10,11]=T. Step 6: All used, return true.

Step 1: Sort: [1,1,2,2,3,3], n=6, 6%3=0. used = [F,F,F,F,F,F]. Step 2: i=0, arr[0]=1 not used, call findSequence start=1, k=3: find 1,2,3 (use indices 0,2,4), mark used[0,2,4]=T. Step 3: i=1, arr[1]=1 not used, call findSequence start=1, k=3: need 1,2,3. Find 1 at index 1, mark used[1]=T. Need 2: scan from index 2, arr[2]=2 but used[2]=T, arr[3]=2 not used, mark used[3]=T. Need 3: scan from index 3, arr[4]=3 but used[4]=T, arr[5]=3 not used, mark used[5]=T. Group formed. Step 4: i=2, arr[2]=2 but used[2]=T, skip. i=3, arr[3]=2 but used[3]=T, skip. i=4, arr[4]=3 but used[4]=T, skip. i=5, arr[5]=3 but used[5]=T, skip. All used, return true? Wait, check: We formed two groups: [1,2,3] and [1,2,3], but groups must be consecutive numbers: first group 1,2,3 is consecutive, second group 1,2,3 is also consecutive. So output should be true? But problem expects false? Let's re-examine: In step 3, when starting from arr[1]=1, we need 1,2,3. We found 1 at index 1, 2 at index 3, 3 at index 5. But are these consecutive? 1,2,3 are consecutive numbers, yes. So why false? Actually, the array [1,1,2,2,3,3] with k=3 can be divided into [1,2,3] and [1,2,3], both sets of 3 consecutive numbers. So expected output should be true. But in the L0 test case, I said false—let's correct: For arr=[1,1,2,2,3,3], k=3, it is true because we can form two groups of 1,2,3. I'll update in L0 test cases. For this trace, output is true. Step 5: All used, return true.

ladders.title: Building Block — Sorting and Linear Search in an Array
ladders.desc: Sort an array and then perform linear searches to find specific elements.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves two fundamental operations: sorting an array to arrange elements in a specific order (here, ascending order), and then performing linear searches to locate elements with certain properties. Sorting is crucial because it groups consecutive numbers together, making it easier to find sequences like 1,2,3,... Without sorting, we would have to search the entire array randomly for each consecutive number, which would be highly inefficient. After sorting, we can scan the array from left to right to find elements in order. Linear search in this context means iterating through the array from a starting index to find the first occurrence of a target value that meets a condition (e.g., not used). Since the array is sorted, if we are looking for a number larger than the current, we can start the search from the last found index to improve efficiency, but in the brute force approach, we often scan from the beginning or current index. The intuition is that by having a sorted array, consecutive numbers will appear in sequence, so when we need to find 'start+1' after finding 'start', we can resume the search from where we left off, reducing the time complexity. However, in the naive implementation, we might scan from the start each time, leading to O(n) per search. Time complexity for sorting is O(n log n) using efficient algorithms like quicksort or mergesort. Linear search takes O(n) in the worst case per search. Space complexity for sorting can be O(1) for in-place sorts or O(n) for auxiliary space. Connection to L2: This building block enables the iterative group formation by providing a sorted array and the ability to find consecutive numbers efficiently. Common mistakes include not using the sorted property to optimize searches, not handling duplicate values correctly during search, and not considering edge cases like empty arrays. Real code pattern: arr.sort(), then for target in sequence: found = False, for i in range(startIndex, len(arr)): if arr[i] == target and not used[i]: found = True, mark used[i], startIndex = i+1, break, if not found: return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort arr: [1,2,3,4]. Step 2: Initialize used = [F,F,F,F], startIndex = 0. Step 3: For target=1: scan from i=0, arr[0]=1, not used, mark used[0]=T, found at index 0, update startIndex=1. Step 4: For target=2: scan from i=1, arr[1]=2, not used, mark used[1]=T, found at index 1, update startIndex=2. Step 5: For target=3: scan from i=2, arr[2]=3, not used, mark used[2]=T, found at index 2, update startIndex=3. Step 6: For target=4: scan from i=3, arr[3]=4, not used, mark used[3]=T, found at index 3, update startIndex=4. All found, indices in original order? But we sorted, so indices refer to sorted array: 0,1,2,3. If we need original indices, we'd track them, but here we just show finding in sorted order.

Step 1: Sort arr: [1,3,5,7]. Step 2: used = [F,F,F,F], startIndex = 0. Step 3: For target=1: scan from i=0, arr[0]=1, not used, mark used[0]=T, found, startIndex=1. Step 4: For target=2: scan from i=1, arr[1]=3 not match, i=2:5, i=3:7, end: target 2 not found. Return false.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Marking Elements as Used in an Array
ladders.desc: Track which elements in an array have been processed by marking them in a separate boolean array.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves maintaining a parallel data structure, typically a boolean array of the same length as the input array, to record whether each element has been used or processed. The intuition is that when we iterate through an array and perform operations on certain elements (like removing them or including them in a group), we need a way to remember which elements are still available for future operations without actually deleting them from the array (which would be costly in terms of time complexity). By using a boolean 'used' array, we can mark an element as used by setting its corresponding index to True, and later checks can quickly see if it's available by checking if used[i] is False. This is efficient because accessing and updating a boolean value is O(1) time. In the context of the divide array problem, after sorting, we scan the array and for each element that is not used, we attempt to form a group. When we include an element in a group, we mark it as used to prevent it from being reused in another group. This ensures that each element is used exactly once. The space complexity for this operation is O(n) for the boolean array. Connection to L3: This operation is essential during the linear search in the sorted array to skip over elements that have already been used. Common mistakes include not initializing the boolean array properly (all False), not updating it correctly when an element is used, and accessing indices out of bounds. Real code pattern: n = len(arr), used = [False] * n, then when element at index i is used: used[i] = True, and to check: if not used[i]: process.

examples.input: arr = [1,2,3,4], used = [F,F,F,F], mark indices 0 and 2 as used
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize used array: [False, False, False, False]. Step 2: Mark index 0 as used: set used[0] = True. Step 3: Mark index 2 as used: set used[2] = True. Result: used = [True, False, True, False].

examples.input: arr = [5,5,5], used = [F,F,F], mark all as used
examples.trace: Step 1: used = [False, False, False]. Step 2: Mark index 0: used[0] = True. Step 3: Mark index 1: used[1] = True. Step 4: Mark index 2: used[2] = True. Result: used = [True, True, True].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Consecutive Numbers and Arrays
ladders.desc: Learn what consecutive numbers are and how to store them in an array.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept. Consecutive numbers are integers that follow each other in order without gaps, such as 1,2,3 or 10,11,12. The difference between each pair of consecutive numbers is always 1. For example, in the sequence 5,6,7, each number is one more than the previous. An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements in a linear order, accessible by index. In programming, we can store numbers in an array, and we can check if they are consecutive by iterating through the array and verifying that each element is exactly one more than the previous element when sorted. For instance, if we have an array [3,1,2], after sorting it becomes [1,2,3], which are consecutive numbers. The intuition for the divide array problem builds on this: we need to group numbers into sets where each set contains k consecutive numbers. This foundation involves basic operations like comparing numbers, understanding increments by 1, and manipulating arrays. Time complexity for checking if an array contains consecutive numbers is O(n log n) if we sort first, or O(n) with additional space using a hash set. Space complexity is O(1) for simple checks. Connection to L4: This concept is used when we mark elements and need to identify sequences of consecutive numbers in the array. Common mistakes include not considering negative numbers or zero, not handling duplicates, and confusing consecutive numbers with other patterns. Real code pattern: To check if arr contains consecutive numbers: sort arr, then for i in range(1, len(arr)): if arr[i] != arr[i-1] + 1: return False, return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort array (already sorted): [1,2,3,4]. Step 2: Check consecutive: start with i=1: arr[1]=2, arr[0]=1, 2 == 1+1 yes. i=2: arr[2]=3, arr[1]=2, 3 == 2+1 yes. i=3: arr[3]=4, arr[2]=3, 4 == 3+1 yes. All consecutive, return true.
Step 1: Sort array: [5,6,7]. Step 2: Check consecutive: i=1: arr[1]=6, arr[0]=5, 6 == 5+1 yes. i=2: arr[2]=7, arr[1]=6, 7 == 6+1 yes. All consecutive, return true.

approaches.name: HashMap and Greedy O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Use a hash map to count frequencies of numbers, sort unique keys, and greedily form groups starting from the smallest available number, decrementing counts as used. This efficiently tracks available elements without repeated scans.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n) due to sorting, O(n) for hash map operations

ladders.title: Full Problem — Divide Array into Sets of K Consecutive Numbers
ladders.desc: Complete solution using HashMap and Greedy approach to check if array can be divided into sets of k consecutive numbers.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires determining if we can partition an array into groups where each group contains exactly k consecutive integers. The HashMap and Greedy approach works by first counting the frequency of each number using a hash map. We then sort the unique numbers to process them in ascending order. For each number, if it has remaining frequency, we attempt to form a group starting from that number. We check if we can find k consecutive numbers (from current number to current + k - 1) where each has at least one available count. If yes, we decrement the counts for all these numbers. This greedy approach works because starting from the smallest available number ensures we don't leave 'gaps' that would prevent forming valid groups later. The intuition is similar to playing cards: you want to form straights starting from the smallest available card. Time complexity is O(n log n) due to sorting the unique keys (worst case O(n log n) when all elements are unique) plus O(n) for hash map operations. Space complexity is O(n) for the hash map. Common mistakes include not checking if k divides n (though not strictly required by problem statement), forgetting to handle duplicate numbers properly, and inefficiently scanning the array multiple times. The connection to L1 is that this full solution uses the key subroutine of forming groups from a starting number.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Form Consecutive Group from Starting Number
ladders.desc: Given a starting number and frequency map, check if k consecutive numbers exist and decrement their counts.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the greedy algorithm. Given a starting number 'start' and a frequency map 'freq', we need to check if we can form a group of k consecutive integers starting from 'start'. Specifically, we check if numbers start, start+1, ..., start+k-1 all have frequency ≥ 1 in the map. If any number is missing or has frequency 0, we return false. If all are available, we decrement the frequency of each of these k numbers by 1. The intuition is that we're 'consuming' one instance of each consecutive number to form a valid group. This works greedily because we always start from the smallest available number, ensuring we don't waste larger numbers that might be needed to complete groups starting from smaller numbers. Time complexity is O(k) for checking and decrementing k numbers. Space complexity is O(1) additional space. Common mistakes include not checking if numbers exist in the map before accessing (leading to KeyError), decrementing frequencies even when the group cannot be formed, and not handling the case where frequency becomes zero (should remove from map or leave as zero). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called repeatedly in the main algorithm for each potential starting number. Connection to L2: This implements the core greedy logic of forming groups.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check start=1: freq[1]=2 ≥ 1 ✓
Step 2: Check start+1=2: freq[2]=1 ≥ 1 ✓
Step 3: Check start+2=3: freq[3]=1 ≥ 1 ✓
Step 4: All checks passed. Decrement frequencies:
  freq[1] = 2-1 = 1
  freq[2] = 1-1 = 0
  freq[3] = 1-1 = 0
Step 5: Return true

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Group Formation with Smallest Available Number
ladders.desc: Process numbers in sorted order, always trying to form groups starting from the smallest number with remaining frequency.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the greedy strategy that makes the algorithm efficient. The core insight is: always try to form a group starting from the smallest number that still has remaining frequency. Why does this work? Imagine we have numbers 1,2,3,3,4,4 and k=3. If we start from the smallest available number (1), we form group [1,2,3]. If we instead started from 3 first, we might form [3,4,5] but 5 doesn't exist, then we're left with [1,2,3] but we've used one 3, so we can't complete the group. By always starting from the smallest, we ensure we don't 'block' smaller numbers that must be used as starters. The algorithm: 1. Sort unique numbers from frequency map. 2. Iterate through sorted numbers. 3. For each number, if its frequency > 0, try to form a group starting from that number (using L1 subroutine). 4. If at any point group formation fails, return false. 5. After processing all numbers, if all frequencies are 0, return true. Time complexity dominated by sorting: O(m log m) where m is number of unique elements. Space: O(m) for sorted list. Common mistakes: not sorting, which leads to incorrect greedy choices; continuing after a failure instead of returning immediately; not checking frequency > 0 before attempting group formation. Connection to L1: This level calls the group formation subroutine. Connection to L3: This uses sorting as a building block.

ladders.title: Building Block — Counting Frequencies with Hash Map
ladders.desc: Create a frequency map (dictionary) counting occurrences of each number in an array.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental building block for many algorithms. A frequency map (hash map, dictionary) stores each unique number as a key and its count as the value. We iterate through the array once: for each number, if it's not in the map, add it with count 1; if it's already in the map, increment its count. The intuition is that we're compressing the array into a summary of what elements exist and how many times they appear. This allows O(1) average time lookups and updates. Time complexity: O(n) where n is array length, since we process each element once. Space complexity: O(m) where m is number of unique elements (worst case O(n) if all elements are unique). Common mistakes: using a list instead of hash map for sparse numbers (wastes space), not handling the first occurrence specially (need to initialize count to 1), or using inefficient data structures. Connection to L2: The frequency map is used as input to the greedy algorithm. Connection to L4: This uses basic hash map operations like get and put.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Hash Map Get and Put/Update
ladders.desc: Retrieve and update values in a hash map (dictionary) with proper handling of missing keys.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental operations needed to work with frequency maps. A hash map (dictionary) stores key-value pairs. The two essential operations are: 1. GET: Retrieve the value associated with a key. If the key doesn't exist, we need to handle this case (often by returning a default value like 0). 2. PUT/UPDATE: Set or update the value for a key. For frequency counting, we typically want to increment the value if key exists, or set it to 1 if it doesn't. The intuition is that we're maintaining a running tally. In Python-style pseudocode, we can use `map.get(key, 0)` to safely get a value (returning 0 if key missing), then `map[key] = map.get(key, 0) + 1` to increment. Alternatively, use `if key in map: map[key] += 1 else: map[key] = 1`. Time complexity: O(1) average for both operations. Space: O(1) per operation. Common mistakes: directly accessing `map[key]` without checking existence (causes KeyError), not initializing properly for first occurrence, or using inefficient patterns. Connection to L3: These operations are used to build the frequency map. Connection to L5: Hash maps build upon the concept of key-value storage.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check if key 5 in map: yes (value is 2)
Step 2: Increment value: 2 + 1 = 3
Step 3: Update map: map[5] = 3
Step 4: Result map = {5:3}

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Key-Value Storage with Dictionaries
ladders.desc: Understanding how dictionaries (hash maps) store and retrieve data using keys and values.
ladders.explanation: This is the most fundamental concept: a dictionary (also called hash map, map, or associative array) is a data structure that stores pairs of keys and values. Each key is unique and maps to a value. Think of it like a real dictionary: the word (key) has a definition (value). In programming, we use dictionaries to quickly look up information. For example, we could store student IDs (keys) mapping to names (values). The key insight is that looking up a value by its key is very fast (typically O(1) average time). Basic operations: 1. Create empty dictionary: `dict = {}`. 2. Add key-value pair: `dict[key] = value`. 3. Get value by key: `value = dict[key]` (but this raises error if key doesn't exist). 4. Check if key exists: `key in dict`. 5. Get with default: `dict.get(key, default_value)` returns the value if key exists, else returns default_value. In our frequency map use case, the key is a number from the array, and the value is how many times it appears. This allows us to answer 'how many times does number X appear?' quickly. Time complexity: insert and lookup are O(1) on average. Space: O(n) for n key-value pairs. Common beginner mistakes: confusing dictionaries with lists (lists use integer indices, dictionaries use any hashable keys), trying to use unhashable types as keys, or not handling missing keys properly. Connection to L4: This concept enables the get/put operations.

examples.input: Create dictionary, add key 10 with value 'apple', then retrieve value for key 10
Dictionary d = {1:'cat',2:'dog'}. Check if key 3 exists, then add key 3 with value 'fish'

approaches.name: Min-Heap and HashMap O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Maintain a min-heap of unique numbers and a hash map of frequencies, repeatedly popping the smallest element to start a group and checking for consecutive numbers using the map. This avoids full sorting by using a heap for dynamic minimum retrieval.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n) for heap operations
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for heap and hash map

ladders.title: Full Problem — Divide Array into Sets of K Consecutive Numbers Using Min-Heap and HashMap
ladders.desc: Complete solution checking if array can be divided into sets of k consecutive numbers using min-heap for dynamic minimum retrieval and hash map for frequency tracking.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the problem by avoiding full sorting while efficiently finding consecutive groups. The intuition is that we need to repeatedly find the smallest available number to start a group, then check if we have k consecutive numbers available. Using a min-heap gives us O(log n) access to the smallest element, while a hash map tracks frequencies for O(1) availability checks. The algorithm works by first counting frequencies and building a min-heap of unique numbers. Then, while the heap isn't empty, we pop the smallest number. If its frequency is zero (meaning it was used in previous groups), we skip it. Otherwise, we attempt to form a group starting from this number, checking for k consecutive numbers in the map. For each number in the group, we decrement its frequency in the map. If any number is missing (frequency zero), we return false. If we successfully form all groups, we return true. Time complexity is O(n log n) due to heap operations (each unique number is pushed/popped once, and we may process each element multiple times for group formation). Space complexity is O(n) for the heap and hash map. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete group formation logic. Common mistakes include not properly handling duplicate numbers (need frequency map), not skipping numbers with zero frequency after popping from heap, and not checking if k consecutive numbers exist before forming a group. Real code pattern: Build frequency map, build min-heap from unique numbers, while heap not empty: pop min, if freq[min] == 0: continue, for i in range(k): if freq[min+i] == 0: return False, freq[min+i] -= 1, if freq[min+i] == 0: remove from heap if present, return True if all groups formed.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build frequency map: {1:1, 2:1, 3:2, 4:2, 5:1, 6:1}. Build min-heap: [1,2,3,4,5,6]. Step 2: Heap not empty, pop min=1, freq[1]=1>0. Form group [1,2,3,4]. Check consecutive: freq[1]=1→decrement to 0, freq[2]=1→0, freq[3]=2→1, freq[4]=2→1. Remove 1,2 from heap (freq 0). Heap now [3,4,5,6]. Step 3: Pop min=3, freq[3]=1>0. Form group [3,4,5,6]. Check: freq[3]=1→0, freq[4]=1→0, freq[5]=1→0, freq[6]=1→0. Remove 3,4,5,6 from heap. Heap empty. All groups formed, return true.

Step 1: Frequency map: {1:1, 2:1, 3:1, 4:1}. Min-heap: [1,2,3,4]. Step 2: Pop min=1, freq[1]=1>0. Form group [1,2,3]. Check: freq[1]=1→0, freq[2]=1→0, freq[3]=1→0. Remove 1,2,3 from heap. Heap now [4]. Step 3: Pop min=4, freq[4]=1>0. Try form group starting at 4: need [4,5,6], but 5 not in map (freq 0). Return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Forming a Consecutive Group from Minimum Element
ladders.desc: Given a starting number and k, check if k consecutive numbers exist in frequency map and decrement their counts.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core operation in the full solution. The intuition is that once we have the smallest available number (from the min-heap), we need to verify that we can form a complete group of k consecutive numbers starting from that number. We use a frequency map (hash map) to track how many copies of each number we have available. For i from 0 to k-1, we check if the number (start + i) exists in the map with frequency > 0. If any number is missing (frequency 0), we cannot form the group and return false. If all numbers are available, we decrement their frequencies by 1 (using one copy of each for this group). Additionally, if a number's frequency becomes zero after decrementing, we might need to remove it from the heap (though heap cleanup is handled in the main algorithm). Time complexity is O(k) for checking k consecutive numbers. Space complexity is O(1) extra space. This connects to L0 by being the critical step repeated in the main loop. Common mistakes include not checking all k numbers before decrementing (which could leave partial groups), not handling the case where start+i might not be in the map at all (should return false), and not properly updating the frequency map. Real code pattern: function form_group(start, k, freq_map): for i in range(k): if freq_map.get(start+i, 0) == 0: return False, freq_map[start+i] -= 1, return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check number 3: freq_map[3]=2>0, decrement to 1. Step 2: Check number 4: freq_map[4]=2>0, decrement to 1. Step 3: Check number 5: freq_map[5]=1>0, decrement to 0. Step 4: Check number 6: freq_map[6]=1>0, decrement to 0. All checks passed, return true. Updated map: {1:1, 2:1, 3:1, 4:1, 5:0, 6:0}.

Step 1: Check number 1: freq_map[1]=1>0, decrement to 0. Step 2: Check number 2: freq_map[2]=1>0, decrement to 0. Step 3: Check number 3: freq_map.get(3,0)=0, return false. Map partially updated: {1:0, 2:0, 4:1}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Using Min-Heap to Dynamically Retrieve Minimum Available Number
ladders.desc: Maintain a min-heap of unique numbers to efficiently get the smallest element without full sorting.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique that makes the approach efficient. The intuition is that we need to repeatedly find the smallest number that still has copies available (frequency > 0) to start new groups. Instead of sorting the entire array (which would be O(n log n) but static), we use a min-heap which allows dynamic operations: we can push all unique numbers initially, then pop the minimum in O(log n) time. When we use a number and its frequency drops to zero, we don't immediately remove it from the heap (as removal is O(n)), but when we pop from the heap, we check if its frequency is zero—if so, we skip it and pop again. This lazy deletion ensures we always get the smallest available number. The heap contains only unique numbers, so its size is at most n. Time complexity: building heap O(m log m) where m is number of unique elements, each pop is O(log m). Space: O(m) for heap. This connects to L1 by providing the starting number for group formation. Common mistakes include pushing all elements (not unique) into the heap (wastes space), not handling skipped elements properly, or trying to remove elements from heap directly (expensive). Real code pattern: Build freq map, heap = list(freq.keys()), heapify(heap), while heap: min_val = heappop(heap), if freq[min_val] == 0: continue, else use min_val to start group.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initial heap (unordered): [3,1,2]. Step 2: Apply heapify: rearranges to min-heap order [1,3,2]. Step 3: First heappop returns 1 (smallest). Step 4: Check freq[1]=1>0, use it. Step 5: After using, freq[1]=0. Next heappop returns 2 (since heap now [2,3] after pop). Check freq[2]=1>0, use it.
Step 1: Heap is [5,6,7] (min-heap). Step 2: heappop returns 5. Step 3: Check freq[5]=0, so skip it. Step 4: Next heappop returns 6. Check freq[6]=1>0, use it. Heap now [7].

ladders.title: Building Block — Counting Frequencies with Hash Map
ladders.desc: Create a frequency map (dictionary) counting occurrences of each number in an array.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for tracking how many copies of each number are available. The intuition is that we need to know not just which numbers exist, but how many times each appears, because we might have duplicates and need to use each copy exactly once. A hash map (dictionary) provides O(1) average time for insert and lookup, making it efficient. We iterate through the array once: for each number, if it's not in the map, add it with count 1; if it's already present, increment its count. This gives us a complete inventory of numbers. Time complexity is O(n) for iterating through n elements. Space complexity is O(m) where m is the number of unique elements (at most n). This connects to L2 by providing the frequency map needed for the heap and group formation. Common mistakes include using an array instead of hash map (inefficient for sparse numbers), not handling negative numbers (hash map works fine), or incorrectly counting (e.g., off-by-one errors). Real code pattern: freq = {}, for num in nums: freq[num] = freq.get(num, 0) + 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize freq={}. Step 2: Process 1: not in map, add {1:1}. Step 3: Process 2: not in map, add {1:1, 2:1}. Step 4: Process 2: already in map, increment to {1:1, 2:2}. Step 5: Process 3: not in map, add {1:1, 2:2, 3:1}. Step 6: Process 3: increment to {1:1, 2:2, 3:2}. Step 7: Process 3: increment to {1:1, 2:2, 3:3}. Final map.
Step 1: freq={}. Step 2: Process 5: add {5:1}. Step 3: Process 5: increment to {5:2}. Step 4: Process 5: increment to {5:3}. Final map.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Heap Push and Pop Operations
ladders.desc: Insert elements into a min-heap and extract the minimum element.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves using a heap data structure to maintain a collection where the smallest element can be efficiently retrieved. A min-heap is a complete binary tree where each node is smaller than or equal to its children. The intuition is that by organizing elements in this tree structure, we can get the minimum in O(1) time (it's always at the root) and insert or remove in O(log n) time by bubbling elements up or down. In practice, heaps are often implemented using arrays. Key operations: push (insert) adds an element to the end and bubbles it up to maintain heap property; pop (extract min) removes the root, replaces it with the last element, and bubbles it down. Time complexity: O(log n) for push and pop, where n is heap size. Space: O(n) to store elements. This connects to L3 by being used to build and maintain the heap of unique numbers. Common mistakes include not maintaining heap property after operations, confusing min-heap with max-heap, or using inefficient implementations. Real code pattern: import heapq, heap = [], heapq.heappush(heap, value), min_val = heapq.heappop(heap).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initial min-heap [5,10,15] (5 is root). Step 2: Push 3: add to end [5,10,15,3]. Bubble up: compare 3 with parent 15, swap → [5,10,3,15]. Compare 3 with parent 5, swap → [3,10,5,15]. Heap property restored. Step 3: Pop: remove root 3, replace with last element 15 → [15,10,5]. Bubble down: compare 15 with children 10 and 5, swap with smaller child 5 → [5,10,15]. Compare 5 with children (10,15), no swap needed. Pop returns 3.

examples.input: Empty heap, push 7, push 2, push 9, then pop twice
examples.trace: Step 1: Heap empty. Step 2: Push 7: heap [7]. Step 3: Push 2: add to end [7,2], bubble up: compare 2 with parent 7, swap → [2,7]. Step 4: Push 9: add to end [2,7,9], bubble up: compare 9 with parent 7, no swap needed. Step 5: First pop: remove root 2, replace with last 9 → [9,7], bubble down: compare 9 with child 7, swap → [7,9]. Pop returns 2. Step 6: Second pop: remove root 7, replace with last 9 → [9], bubble down: no children. Pop returns 7.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Consecutive Numbers
ladders.desc: Identify and work with sequences where each number is exactly one more than the previous.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: consecutive numbers are integers that follow each other in order without gaps. For example, 3,4,5 are consecutive because 4 = 3+1 and 5 = 4+1. The intuition is that in many problems, we need to check for or create such sequences. Key properties: the difference between consecutive numbers is always 1; if we start at a number x, the next consecutive numbers are x+1, x+2, etc.; sequences can be of any length. This foundational idea is used in the problem to define what a 'set of k consecutive numbers' means. Time and space are trivial for small sequences. This connects to L4 by being the basic pattern we check when forming groups. Common mistakes include confusing consecutive with other patterns (like even numbers), not handling negative numbers correctly (e.g., -2,-1,0 are consecutive), or off-by-one errors in sequence length. Real code pattern: To check if list [a,b,c] is consecutive: for i in range(len(list)-1): if list[i+1] != list[i] + 1: return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with 7. Step 2: Check next number 8: 8 == 7+1? Yes. Step 3: Check next number 9: 9 == 8+1? Yes. Step 4: All checks pass, so true.
Step 1: Start with 1. Step 2: Check next number 3: 3 == 1+1? No (2 expected). Step 3: Already false, so return false.
